steps.title: Identify the requirement of the communication claim
steps.content: The second team's claim that sounds primarily communicate with other members depends on one thing: the sound-producing caterpillars must actually encounter other members to communicate with. If they don't meet this requirement, the claim falls apart.

steps.title: Find what undermines the communication requirement
steps.content:
Choice D (‘Caterpillars that were found to produce sounds in response to simulated attacks are typically solitary and were tested in isolation.’) shows that caterpillars producing these sounds are typically solitary and tested in isolation—meaning they don't encounter other members of their species in the first place.

If they're naturally solitary, they have no other caterpillars to communicate with, making intraspecies communication unlikely as a primary function.

The other options don't address this central issue: A and B describe properties of sounds or behavior in other species, while C only explains when sounds occur, not whether the producing caterpillar has anyone to communicate with.

That confirms D is correct.
